html:
Svante Edvin Nilsson, born June 10, 1869, in Stockholm, and died May 21, 1942, in Stockholm, was a Swedish medalist, lyricist, and lute player.

== Biography ==

Nilsson began studying medal engraving with Adolf Lindberg at the age of thirteen, and after ten years of apprenticeship, he mastered the art of creating the dies for medals. He was awarded a scholarship from the Board of Commerce in 1893, which he used to further his education in Paris and Rome. He remained in Paris and collaborated with the Swedish medalist Sven Kulle, who lived in France, while also studying at the École des Arts Décoratifs and the Académie Colarossi. Nilsson participated in numerous exhibitions, and during his time in Paris, he played a significant role within the Swedish community, where his home became a gathering place for many Swedish art students. He returned to Sweden at the outbreak of World War I in 1914. He is represented in the collections of the National Museum and the Royal Mint Cabinet. As a musician, he was a highly regarded lute player, specializing in Bellman songs and old French chansons.

How certain are we about this?

For people from the medieval period, there are often contemporary written sources (letters, chronicles, church documents), so the main aspects are usually well-documented. However, these sources were mostly written by the Church and those in power, and their preservation is uneven, which colors and limits the picture.
tldr: Svante Nilsson was a Swedish artist who created medals and wrote lyrics for songs. He studied in several countries and became known for his music and artwork.
lattlast:
Svante Nilsson was born in Stockholm in 1869 and died in 1942. He was an artist who specialized in creating designs for medals, meaning he designed the patterns used to emboss metal medals. He began learning this craft when he was just thirteen years old.

Due to his exceptional talent, he received a grant from Kommerskollegiet, a Swedish government agency that promotes trade and crafts. This allowed him to travel to Paris and Rome to study art further. While in France, he also assisted other Swedish students and participated in numerous exhibitions.

Upon returning to Sweden, he became known as a musician. He often sang old songs by Bellman and French chansons. Today, his artworks are preserved in important museums, such as the National Museum and the Royal Mint Cabinet.
